paper_title: Why Does Self-Distillation (Sometimes) Degrade the Reasoning Capability of LLMs?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Self-distillation has emerged as an effective post-training paradigm for LLMs, often improving performance while shortening reasoning traces. However, in mathematical reasoning, we find that it can reduce response length while degrading performance. We trace this degradation to the suppression of epistemic verbalization - the model's expression of uncertainty during reasoning. Through controlled experiments varying conditioning context richness and task coverage, we show that conditioning the teacher on rich information suppresses uncertainty expression, enabling rapid in-domain optimization with limited task coverage but harming OOD performance, where unseen problems benefit from expressing uncertainty and adjusting accordingly. Across Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-7B, and Olmo3-7B-Instruct, we observe performance drops of up to 40%. Our findings highlight that exposing appropriate levels of uncertainty is crucial for robust reasoning and underscore the importance of optimizing reasoning behavior beyond merely reinforcing correct answer traces.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 5 2 ] . [ 1 2 7 4 4 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r Preprint. Under review. Why Does Self-Distillation (Sometimes) Degrade the Reasoning Capability of LLMs? Jeonghye Kim1,2, Xufang Luo1, Minbeom Kim3, Sangmook Lee3, Dohyung Kim3, Jiwon Jeon2, Dongsheng Li1, Yuqing Yang1 1Microsoft Research 2KAIST 3Seoul National University blog post beanie00/self-distillation-analysis

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Self-distillation has emerged as an effective post-training paradigm for LLMs, often improving performance while shortening reasoning traces. However, in mathematical reasoning, we find that it can reduce response length while degrading performance. We trace this degradation to the suppression of epistemic verbalizationthe models expression of uncertainty during reasoning. Through controlled experiments varying conditioning context richness and task coverage, we show that conditioning the teacher on rich information suppresses uncertainty expression, enabling rapid in-domain optimization with limited task coverage but harming OOD performance, where unseen problems benefit from expressing uncertainty and adjusting accordingly. Across Qwen3-8B, DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-7B, and Olmo3-7B-Instruct, we observe performance drops of up to 40%. Our findings highlight that exposing appropriate levels of uncertainty is crucial for robust reasoning and underscore the importance of optimizing reasoning behavior beyond merely reinforcing correct answer traces.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Recently, self-distillation (Snell et al., 2022) has attracted increasing attention in the posttraining of large language models (LLMs). In this paradigm, two instances of the same model are employed: one conditioned on the ground-truth solutions serves as teacher, providing informative reward signals for responses generated by another instance that does not have access to the solutions. Several studies have demonstrated that combining this framework with post-training methods such as Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) leads to highly efficient performance gains (Zhu et al., 2025; Kim et al., 2026; ubotter et al., 2026; Shenfeld et al., 2026; Song et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2026; Ye et al., 2026). These methods have shown particularly strong improvements in domains such (a) Chemistry (Olmo3-7B-Instruct) (b) DAPO-Math-17k (DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-7B) Figure 1: (a) Training score and response length changes for GRPO and Self-Distillation (SDPO) (H ubotter et al., 2026) in Chemistry, using results from SDPO Wandb logs (Biewald, 2020) (link). (b) Training score and response length changes on DAPO-Math-17k with GRPO and SDPO. Work done during an internship at Microsoft Research. Corresponding author. Preprint. Under review. as agentic environments and scientific reasoning, especially under in-domain evaluation settings. Interestingly, consistent trend observed across these works is that performance improves as response length decreases, suggesting that self-distillation promotes more concise and effective reasoning. However, when we apply the same self-distillation approach to mathematical reasoning tasks, we observe markedly different phenomenon. Figure 1 compares the effects of representative self-distillation algorithm, SDPO, in the Chemistry domain (a) and the Math domain (b). As shown in the figure, in the Chemistry domain, self-distillation substantially reduces response length compared to GRPO while rapidly improving performance. In contrast, in the Math domain, although response length consistently decreases as training progresses, performance drops significantly, contrary to prior findings. This raises an question: Why does performance sometimes degrade despite the model being trained to move toward the correct answer? Our analysis reveals consistent pattern: the more informative the context provided to the teacher, the more concise and confident the resulting reasoning becomes, with substantially fewer expressions of uncertainty and, particularly in math reasoning, degraded performance. We trace this effect to the suppression of epistemic verbalization (Kim et al., 2026), whereby models explicitly verbalize and incorporate uncertainty during reasoning. Strong reasoning models such as DeepSeek-R1 (Guo et al., 2025b) frequently express uncertainty using tokens like Wait or Hmm. Although these expressions may not directly advance the reasoning, removing them discards important signals that reasoning path may be flawed, leading to significant performance drops (Kim et al., 2026). To systematically understand when and why self-distillation suppresses epistemic verbalization, we conduct comprehensive empirical study and identify two key factors: information richness and task coverage. When the teacher is conditioned on richer information, such as the correct solution, it produces reasoning trajectories with little expressed uncertainty, encouraging the student to imitate confident reasoning style that presupposes information unavailable at inference time. When task coverage is limited, this compression enables rapid in-domain optimization. However, as coverage increases, the trained removal of epistemic verbalization can interfere with optimization across diverse tasks, degrading performance on more challenging or previously unseen problems. More broadly, our results show that even when the training objective faithfully guides the model toward correct reasoning traces, the resulting reasoning style can quietly shift in ways that hurt generalization. The suppression of epistemic verbalization is not penalized by standard objectives, yet negatively impacts out-of-distribution (OOD) performance. This suggests that post-training objectives need to account not only for answer correctness, but also for eliciting and preserving uncertainty-aware reasoning behaviors. We believe these findings offer useful step toward deeper understanding of reasoning in self-distillation and post-training more broadly.

sections.title: 2 Preliminaries
sections.content: Let denote an input and = (y1, . . . , yT) sequence generated Self-Distillation by language model πθ. The model defines an autoregressive distribution πθ(yx) = t=1 πθ(yt x, y<t). In self-distillation, the same model acts as both student and teacher under different conditioning contexts. The student first generates sequence πθ( x). The teacher policy is obtained by conditioning the model on richer context that provides additional information about the input (e.g., solutions, environment feedback, or other auxiliary signals): πT θ ( x, c) = πθ( x, c). Training minimizes the divergence between the student and teacher next-token distributions: LSD(θ) = KL(cid:0)πθ( x, y<t) stopgrad(cid:0)πθ( x, c, y<t)(cid:1)(cid:1) . (1) This objective encourages the student to match the teachers predictions under the richer context, enabling the model to improve by distilling information available at training time without requiring an external teacher. Preprint. Under review. Key Characteristics of Math Reasoning In LLMs, math reasoning can be viewed as self-Bayesian reasoning, where each step is generated conditioned only on the problem and previously generated tokens, with the model iteratively updating its belief over intermediate hypotheses (Kim et al., 2026). At the same time, math reasoning spans diverse tasks such as arithmetic, algebra, geometry, word problems, and logical pattern recognition, making evaluation benchmarks frequently OOD relative to training data due to compositional and reasoning-depth shifts. deeper discussion on task coverage, its impact on performance, and how this distinguishes math from other domains is provided in Section 6. (2a) Reasoning with Epistemic Verbalization (2b) Unguided vs. Teacher-Guided Within this process, verbalized uncertainty toward yreferred to as epistemic verbalization (Kim et al., 2026)can serve as an informative signal rather than mere stylistic redundancy. As illustrated in Figure 2(2a), reasoning without such signals may lead the model to prematurely commit to incorrect hypotheses with limited opportunity for recovery, whereas epistemic verbalization helps maintain alternative hypotheses and supports gradual uncertainty reduction. In self-distillation, the teacher has access to richer context c, enabling it to generate reasoning trajectories with strong hints and minimal expressed uncertainty. While this leads to more concise responses, it may hinder the students ability to perform uncertainty-aware reasoning. Consequently, aggressive length constraints and overly confident reasoning styles risk eliminating not only unnecessary verbosity but also valuable epistemic signals, especially in smaller models with limited parametric knowledge. The key challenge is to filter out non-informative content while retaining epistemic expressions that enable iterative belief refinement, rather than blindly compressing the reasoning process.

sections.title: 3 LLM Reasoning Behavior Under Richer Information
sections.content: Before analyzing self-distillation in depth, we examine how LLM reasoning changes when richer information is provided. To formalize the informativeness of the conditioning context, we define the information that provides about the target sequence as the conditional mutual information I(y; x) = H(y x) H(y x, c), (2) which captures the reduction in uncertainty about once the additional context is given. Using the DAPO-Math-17k dataset (Yu et al., 2025) and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (Guo et al., 2025b) as the base model, we select 100 problems on which the base model achieves accuracy between 0.125 and 0.5 over 8 rollouts. Let denote the full solution (including chain-of-thought in <think> tags), sthink the solution with <think> content removed, and response previously generated under full solution guidance. We compare the models responses across four generation settings with increasing conditioning information: (1) Unguided generation: = , so I(y; x) = 0 by definition. (2) Solution-guided generation: = s, providing maximal guidance and yielding the largest I(y; x). (3) Solution-guided generation (without think contents): = sthink. Since sthink is strict informational subset of s, we have I(y; sthink x) I(y; x). (4) Regeneration-conditioned generation: = y, where is generated under setting (2). By the data processing inequality, I(y; x) I(y; x). These settings induce the following ordering over the conditional mutual information: I(y; x) = 0 (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) (cid:124) (1) < I(y; sthink x) (cid:125) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (3) 3 I(y; x) (cid:125) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (4) I(y; x) (cid:125) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (2) . (3) Preprint. Under review. Prompts For regeneration, we used the same prompts as in ubotter et al. (2026). The prompts used for unguided and solution-guided settings are as follows. Prompt for unguided generation Regeneration prompt (followed the prompt in ubotter al. (2026)) et {question} Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within boxed{}. {question} Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within boxed{}. Correct solution: {previously correct solution} Correctly solve the original question. Epistemic tokens Following Kim et al. (2026), we define set of 10 epistemic markers = {wait, hmm, perhaps, maybe, actually, alternatively, seems, might, likely, check} as practical indicators of regions where uncertainty externalization may occur. We measure the epistemic token count of response as E(y) = tT count(t, y). Results We analyze how different forms of solution guidance affect the models reasoning behavior by comparing the average response length E[L(y)], model score, and the epistemic token count E[E(y)] across the four settings. As shown in Table 1, both quantities decrease monotonically as I(y; x) increases: E(cid:2)L(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(1) > E(cid:2)L(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(2) > E(cid:2)L(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(4) > E(cid:2)L(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(3) (4) , and analogously for E[E(y)], confirming that richer conditioning information leads to more concise and confident reasoning. Specifically, unguided generation (c = ) produces substantially longer responses with the highest epistemic token counts. When the full solution is provided in (2), the model follows the given reasoning trajectory with high confidence, and its concise output can be viewed as compressed representation of the essential reasoning in s. In (3), removing the <think> portion retains only sthink (640 out of 13,054 response tokens), and both E[L(y)] and E[E(y)] increase again toward the unguided level, reflecting the substantial information loss. Setting (4), conditioning on the regenerated response y, yields intermediate valueslower than (3) but higher than (2)indicating that preserves much of the informative structure of the full solution. Detailed per-token breakdowns are reported in Appendix A.1.1. Table 1: Comparison of response characteristics under varying levels of rich information. (1) Unguided (2) Solution-Guided (c = s) (3) Solution-Guided (c = sthink) (4) Regeneration-Conditioned Avg. Score 0.30 0.98 0.78 0.95 Avg. Length 13,054 1,873 12,036 2,808 Epistemic Token Count 182.5 8.8 159.8 24. Takeaway 1: Information Richness and Epistemic Verbalization As the conditioning context becomes more informative and directly useful, the LLM generates answers more confidently and with fewer expressions of epistemic uncertainty.

sections.title: 4 Supervised Finetuning with Self-Distillation
sections.content: A natural follow-up question is whether the suppression of epistemic verbalization under high I(y; x) is merely stylistic or has tangible impact on reasoning capability. To test this, we conduct off-policy self-distillation (SFT) using DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (Guo et al., 2025a) on two datasets, each containing 800 correct responses: Dug: unguided responses (c = ), with high E[E(y)] and E[L(y)] 12k tokens. 4 Preprint. Under review. Dsg: solution-guided responses (c = s), with low E[E(y)] and E[L(y)] 2k tokens. Both datasets consist entirely of correct trajectories; the key difference lies in the epistemic density of the training signal. We evaluate the resulting checkpoints across multiple math benchmarks (examples from each dataset are presented in our blog). Table 2: Math benchmark performance of the base model DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and its SFT checkpoints trained on unguided and solution-guided datasets. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B Base SFT on Dug SFT on Dsg AIME24 54.79 51.04 20.21 AIME25 37.92 40.00 12.71 AMC23 89.06 87.66 57.03 MATH500 92.19 90.93 65.52 As shown in Table 2, training on Dsg leads to substantial degradation across all benchmarks, despite the dataset consisting of correct answers, whereas training on Dug produces no significant performance change. This asymmetry arises because solution-guided responses are concise precisely due to the external context s; using them as SFT targets without forces the model to imitate reasoning style that presupposes information unavailable at inference time, effectively suppressing the epistemic tokens that support autonomous exploration and error correction. These results are consistent with Kim et al. (2026), which shows that suppressing epistemic verbalization significantly degrades reasoning performance. Takeaway 2: Epistemic Suppression and Reasoning Performance Even when trained on correct trajectories, excessively suppressing epistemic verbalization can substantially degrade reasoning performance.

sections.title: 5 On-Policy Self-Distillation
sections.content: We now turn to on-policy self-distillation (H ubotter et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2026; Ye et al., 2026), in which the model learns from reward signals provided by self-teacher with access to the correct solution, based on responses from the current policy. Concretely, we compare GRPO with Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation (SDPO) (H ubotter et al., 2026) on the DAPO-Math-17k dataset (Yu et al., 2025), using Qwen3-8B (Yang et al., 2025) and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (Guo et al., 2025b) as base models. (Additional results from Olmo-3-7B-Instruct Olmo et al. (2025) can be found in Appendix D.2.). For each model, we track training score and response length, as well as out-of-distribution (OOD) performance on two standard math benchmarks: AIME24 and AMC23. We fix the teacher policy to the initial policy rather than using moving target, as this yields better performance (see Section 5.4 for comparison). The behavior of on-policy self-distillation depends on two factors: (i) how much the base model already shows epistemic verbalization, and (ii) the richness of the conditioning context c. To disentangle these, we compare GRPO and SDPO under two settings: = (full solution) and = sthink (solution without <think> content). 5.1 DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B serves as representative high-reasoning model, which is known for generating extensive epistemic verbalizations within <think> tags and producing lengthy responses, achieving strong reasoning performance. As shown in Figure 4a, GRPO training slightly increases E[L(y)] Training Performance with modest improvement in score. In contrast, SDPO with = causes sharp initial drop in both E[L(y)] and score; performance gradually recovers but remains below GRPO throughout training. When the conditioning is reduced to = sthink, the drop in E[L(y)] is Preprint. Under review. (a) Training Score-Length Comparison (b) AMC23 Score and Response Length (c) AIME24 Score and Response Length (d) Change in Epistemic Token Usage on AIME24 Figure 3: On-policy self-distillation results for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B. GRPO yields modest OOD gains with slight increase in epistemic verbalization, whereas SDPO degrades both performance and epistemic token usage, particularly with = s. attenuated and the score trajectory approaches that of GRPO, consistent with the relationship between I(y; x) and epistemic suppression discussed in Section 3. OOD Evaluation - AIME24, AMC23 Consistent with the training trends, GRPO yields modest gains on both OOD benchmarks (AIME24: 54.7 56.0; AMC23: 89.3 91.1, Figures 3b and 3c) with slight increase in E[L(y)]. SDPO with = degrades performance substantially (40% on AIME24, 15% on AMC23). Reducing the conditioning to = sthink mitigates the drop, though performance still remains below the base model. Reasoning Pattern Figure 3d illustrates the epistemic token counts of the trained models. GRPO increases E[E(y)], whereas SDPO suppresses it more aggressively, which is consistent with the correlation between epistemic suppression and performance degradation observed throughout our analysis. 5.2 Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: ON) With thinking mode enabled, Qwen3-8B initially generates very long responses, even longer than those of DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, along with high number of epistemic tokens, as shown in Appendix A.1.2. As shown in Figure 4a, E[L(y)] decreases under both GRPO and Training Performance SDPO, with SDPO exhibiting larger reduction and correspondingly larger performance drop. Notably, E[L(y)] first drops sharply then increases slightly. Since the teacher policy is fixed as the reference policy, shortening the response by 900 tokens reduces the informativeness of c, i.e., decreases I(y; x). As the context becomes less informative, the model compensates by increasing epistemic verbalization, causing the length to partially recover. OOD Evaluation - AIME24, AMC23 The gap becomes more pronounced on OOD benchmarks: GRPO maintains largely stable performance with gradually decreasing E[L(y)], whereas SDPO falls below the base model, particularly with = s. Notably, although GRPO and SDPO with = sthink achieve comparable training performance, their OOD results divergeespecially on the more challenging AIME24, where SDPO with = sthink shows progressive performance degradation as training proceeds. Both methods reduce E[E(y)] relative to the base model, with SDPO Reasoning Pattern more aggressively so. This suggests that Qwen3-8B originally generates more epistemic ver6 Preprint. Under review. (a) Training Score-Length Comparison (b) AMC23 Score and Response Length (c) AIME24 Score and Response Length (d) Change in Epistemic Token Usage on AIME24 Figure 4: On-policy self-distillation results for Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: ON). Both GRPO and SDPO reduce response length and epistemic verbalization, but SDPOs more aggressive suppression leads to greater OOD performance degradation, particularly on AIME24. balization than necessary; while both methods mitigate this redundancy, overly aggressive suppression risks removing epistemic signals that carry useful reasoning information. 5.3 Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: OFF) (a) Training Score-Length Comparison (b) AIME24 Score and Response Length Figure 5: On-policy self-distillation results for Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: OFF). GRPO rapidly increases response length via epistemic verbalization and achieves strong training gains, while SDPO reduces response length and struggles to improve, with slight OOD degradation on AIME24. When Qwen3-8B is used without thinking mode, the <think> tag is absent, and we compare only = s. The model initially produces much shorter responses and exhibits significantly lower performance. GRPO rapidly increases E[L(y)] by promoting epistemic verbalization (as shown in Appendix D.1), quickly achieving high training score. In contrast, SDPO reduces E[L(y)] and improves much more slowly; even when the training score slightly increases, as shown in Figure 5b, performance on AIME24 slightly declines (0.25 0.23), further illustrating the cost of epistemic suppression under self-distillation. Takeaway 3: Epistemic Verbalization Changes and Performance in On-Policy Self-Distillation As the teacher context becomes more information-rich, on-policy self-distillation reduces epistemic verbalization and shortens responses. This effect varies depending on the level of uncertainty originally expressed by the base model. 5.4 Ablation Study: Fixed vs. Moving Target Teacher In naive on-policy self-distillation, the teacher and student share continuously updated policy, making the teacher moving target that can introduce training instability (Zhao 7 Preprint. Under review. et al., 2026; Ye et al., 2026). To mitigate this, SDPO uses an EMA-smoothed teacher (EMA rate: 0.05). However, we find that setting the EMA rate to 0.0 (i.e., fixing the teacher to the initial policy) yields better performance; therefore, Section 5 follows this setting. (a) DeepSeek-Distill-7B Training Comparison (b) DeepSeek-Distill-7B AIME24 Comparison Figure 6: Fixed vs. moving target teacher for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B. Even slow EMA updates (rate 0.05) amplify epistemic suppression via feedback loop, causing greater performance degradation than fixed teacher. Figure 6a shows additional comparison results when the teacher is updated during training. As shown, even slow updates (e.g., rate 0.05) lead to sharper reduction in response length, resulting in larger performance degradation. This can be interpreted as feedback loop in self-distillation: the model is trained to produce increasingly confident outputs, and when checkpoint of the same model is used as the teacher, it generates even more confident responses, amplifying the effect over iterations. Further ablations on learning rate and top-k logits are in Appendix E.

sections.title: Generalization Ability
sections.content: Across the off-policy and on-policy settings analyzed above, self-distillation consistently produces more confident responses with reduced E[E(y)]. This aligns with the findings of ubotter et al. (2026), which reports that SDPO learns to reason concisely: on Science Q&A (Chemistry, Physics, Biology, and Materials Science) (Feng et al., 2024), tool use (Tang et al., 2023), and LiveCodeBench v6 (Jain et al., 2025), SDPO achieves higher accuracy than GRPO while producing substantially shorter outputs with fewer epistemic markers. In other words, in these domains, self-distillation suppresses epistemic verbalization and improves performance simultaneously. The key question is why the same mechanism leads to performance degradation in our math-focused setup. We hypothesize that the answer lies in differences in task coverage between the training and evaluation distributions. 6.1 Comparison of Task Coverage To test this hypothesis, we compare the dataset characteristics of the settings where SDPO outperformed GRPO against our experimental setup. As shown in Table 3, the Chemistry dataset, despite its large size, draws from only six main problem types that differ primarily in surface details rather than underlying structure. LiveCodeBench v6 contains diverse problems but only 131 in total, leading to repeated exposure during training with identical train/eval splits. In contrast, DAPO-Math-17k exposes the model to 14,000 distinct problems (78% of the 25,600 samples drawn over 100 steps, due to repeated sampling), spanning broad, non-overlapping range of problem types, and evaluation is performed on unseen problem types. 6.2 Relationship Between Task Coverage and Learning Performance To further investigate the interplay between task coverage and generalization, we vary the number of training questions 1, 8, 64, 128, 512 from DAPO-Math-17k and train with both GRPO and SDPO. All experiments use Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode OFF). 8 Preprint. Under review. Table 3: Comparison of the total number of problems, problem composition, and trainevaluation splits in ScienceQ&A, LiveCodeBench v6, and our experiments. Domain ScienceQ&A (Chemistry) LiveCodeBench Dataset Analysis Total 2,400 questions: Reaction Balancing (300), Molecular Descriptor Counting (300), Molecular Weight Calculation (600), Property Prediction (e.g., logS) (500), Precursor/Reactant Selection (Retrosynthesis) (300), Product Prediction (Organic Reactions) (400). Train/Eval split: 90% for training, 10% for evaluation. Train/Eval split: Total 131 questions for training and all 131 questions are used for both training and evaluation. Only 50% of the public test cases are used during training, while the full set including hidden test cases is used for evaluation. DAPO-Math-17k Train/Eval split: Total 14,000 questions for training and evaluation is conducted on standard benchmarks (AIME 2024/2025, AMC 2023, MATH 500) using questions not part of the training data. GRPO and SDPO exhibit distinct training dynamics as varies. When Training Logs 128, SDPO quickly achieves high scores while reducing E[L(y)] by up to 8, indicating higher training efficiency on small task set. However, at = 512, further reductions in E[L(y)] begin to hurt the training score relative to GRPO, whose E[L(y)] gradually increases with D. Figure 7: Training score and response length comparison between GRPO and SDPO for {1, 8, 64, 128, 512}. This difference can be interpreted through task coverage. As grows, the model must accommodate broader range of reasoning patterns. GRPO addresses this by increasing E[E(y)], allowing the model to express greater uncertainty and adapt its reasoning accordingly. SDPO instead encourages confident, concise responseseffective when task coverage is small but limiting when the problem set becomes larger and more diverse. OOD Evaluation - AIME24, MATH500 The distinction between GRPO and SDPO becomes more pronounced on OOD benchmarks (Figure 8). Under GRPO, performance scales consistently with D: = 1 converges quickly but soon stops improving, while larger yields progressively higher final scores accompanied by increasing E[L(y)]. Under SDPO, the pattern reversessmaller leads to more severe OOD degradation. Even at the largest (DAPO setting), SDPO still underperforms the base model. Example reasoning patterns are provided in Appendix A.2. Figure 8: Evaluation performance on AIME24 and MATH500 and response length as varies over {1, 8, 64, 128, 512}. Takeaway 4: Epistemic Verbalization Depends on Task Generalization The value of epistemic verbalization scales with generalization demands: largely redundant for familiar, repetitive tasks (small D) and removable for efficiency, but increasingly important as task diversity grows. 9 Preprint. Under review.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we provide an information-theoretic perspective on self-distillation, recently popular LLM post-training method. Our analysis shows that the effectiveness of selfdistillation depends on how information is provided to the model and how the model incorporates uncertainty into its reasoning process. We find that self-distillation reshapes the models reasoning behavior by encouraging it to produce answers with higher confidence. While this effect enables more compact reasoning and can quickly improve in-domain performance when task coverage is limited, it becomes less effective when task coverage is broad and may even harm out-of-distribution performance. We hope that our analysis contributes to deeper understanding of self-distillation and other LLM post-training methods, and provides insights for developing more robust training strategies.

sections.title: A Additional Analysis of Epistemic Tokens Count
sections.content: A.1 LLM Reasoning Behavior Under Richer Information A.1.1 Per-Token Analysis of Epistemic Verbalization In Table 1 of Section 3, we compared the average number of ten epistemic tokens per response. Figure AA further extends this analysis by examining how the average perresponse count of each individual token changes under varying levels of conditioning information. When examining the per-token counts, all tokens exhibit consistent trend: E(cid:2)E(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(1) > E(cid:2)E(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(3) > E(cid:2)E(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(4) > E(cid:2)E(y)(cid:3)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)(2) , (5) where tokens such as wait, maybe, and perhaps are particularly prominent. Figure 9: Per-token breakdown of epistemic token usage across the four generation settings. Each bar represents the average number of occurrences per response for an individual epistemic token. All tokens follow the same ordering as the aggregate trend, with wait, maybe, and perhaps showing the largest variation across settings. A.1.2 Comparison of Epistemic Token Usage Across Models Following the analysis of DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (DeepSeek-Distill-7B) in Section 3, we further compare epistemic token usage across three settings: DeepSeek-Distill-7B, Qwen3-8B with thinking mode enabled, and Qwen3-8B with thinking mode disabled. As shown in Figure 10, both DeepSeek-Distill-7B and Qwen3-8B with thinking mode enabled produce substantially more epistemic tokens than Qwen3-8B with thinking mode disabled. While the two thinking-enabled models share similar tendency to express uncertainty, they differ in their preferred epistemic tokens. For instance, DeepSeek-Distill-7B frequently uses wait and employs perhaps and maybe at comparable rates, whereas Qwen3-8B uses perhaps relatively less and favors maybe. Qwen3-8B also uses alternatively and check far more than DeepSeek-Distill-7B, and overall embeds greater amount of uncertainty within its reasoning. Extending the discussion in Section 3, we also observe that Qwen3-8B generates far fewer epistemic tokens under solution-guided generation than under unguided generation. Across all three settings, Qwen3-8B with thinking mode enabled produces the most epistemic tokens, followed by DeepSeek-Distill-7B, and then Qwen3-8B with thinking mode disabled. 12 Preprint. Under review. Figure 10: Comparison of epistemic token usage across DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, Qwen3-8B (thinking enabled), and Qwen3-8B (thinking disabled). A.2 Relationship Between Task Coverage and Learning Performance To provide more in-depth analysis of the results in Figure 8 (Section 6.2), we compare the change in epistemic token counts relative to the base model on AIME24 across six training configurations: GRPO and SDPO, each with {1, 64, 512}. Figure 11 shows that GRPO tends to increase epistemic token usage relative to the base model as grows, whereas SDPO reduces epistemic token usage, with smaller leading to greater reduction. Figure 11: Change in epistemic token counts relative to the base model on AIME24 for GRPO and SDPO with {1, 64, 512}.

sections.title: B Experimental Details
sections.content: For GRPO and SDPO training, we built upon the SDPO implementation (https: Training //github.com/lasgroup/SDPO) and additionally incorporated the DAPO-Math-17k dataset. The original DAPO-Math-17k dataset uses the following prompt format: Solve the following math problem step by step. The last line of your response should be of the form Answer: $Answer (without quotes) where $Answer is the answer to the problem.nn{question}nRemember to put your answer on its own line after "Answer:". We replaced this with simpler format: {question}nPlease reason step by step, and put your final answer within boxed{}. 13 Preprint. Under review. as we observed that this format consistently yielded higher evaluation performance. For reward verification, we used the scoring function from the verl framework, which extracts the answer from the boxed{} expression and verifies correctness via exact match followed by mathematical equivalence checking using math-verify, adapted from EleutherAIs lmevaluation-harness (Gao et al., 2024). The GRPO and SDPO training hyperparameters are listed in Tables 4, 5, and 6. For the experiments on the relationship between task coverage and learning performance in Figure 8, we reduced the question batch size to 64 due to the use of smaller training questions. Table 4: Common hyperparameters shared by GRPO and SDPO. Category Parameter Data Batching Rollout Training Max. prompt length Max. response length Question batch size Mini batch size Number of rollouts Inference engine Temperature Optimizer Warmup steps Weight decay Gradient clip norm Table 5: GRPO-specific hyperparameters. Category Parameter Loss ϵ-high Rollout IS clip KL coefficient (λ) Training Learning rate Value 2048 20480 256 64 or 128 8 vllm 1. AdamW 10 0.01 1.0 Value 0.28 2 0.0 1 106 Table 6: SDPO-specific hyperparameters. Category Parameter Loss Distillation divergence Top-K distillation EMA update rate Training Learning rate Value JensenShannon 100 0.0 1 10 14 Preprint. Under review. Evaluation We evaluated all models using the hyperparameters listed in Table 7, following the recommended settings from each models official documentation., Table 7: Evaluation hyperparameters. Model Max Tokens Temp. Top-p Top-K DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B Qwen3-8B (thinking) Qwen3-8B (non-thinking) OLMo-3-7B-Instruct 38912 38912 38912 38912 0.6 0.6 0.7 0. 0.95 0.95 0.8 0.95 20 20 20 20 Chat Templates for Different Model Series Here, we summarize the chat template formats used by several open-weight language model families. Each model series uses distinct special tokens and structures to delineate user and assistant turns. The same math problem is used throughout as an example prompt. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B < begin_of_sentence > < User > Find the largest possible real part of [(75+117 i)z + frac {96+144 }{ }] where is complex number with =4. Please reason step by step , and put your final answer within boxed {}. < Assistant >< think > Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: ON) < im_start > user Find the largest possible real part of [(75+117 i)z + frac {96+144 }{ }] where $z$ is complex number with $z =4 $. Please reason step by step , and put your final answer within boxed {}. < im_end > < im_start > assistant Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: OFF) < im_start > user Find the largest possible real part of [(75+117 i)z + frac {96+144 }{ }] where $z$ is complex number with $z =4 $. Please reason step by step , and put your final answer within boxed {}. < im_end > < im_start > assistant < think > </ think > OLMo-3-7B-Instruct < im_start > system You are helpful function - calling AI assistant . You do not currently have access to any functions . < functions > </ functions > < im_end > < im_start > user Find the largest possible real part of [(75+117 i)z + frac {96+144 }{ }] where $z$ is complex number with $z =4 $. Please reason step by step , and put your final answer within boxed {}. < im_end > < im_start > assistant

sections.title: C Comparison with OPSD
sections.content: Recently, OPSD (Zhao et al., 2026) demonstrated performance gains in mathematical reasoning through self-distillation, particularly on the Qwen3 series. Unlike our setup, where both the student and the teacher either enable or disable thinking mode, OPSD adopts hybrid configuration in which the student operates with thinking mode disabled while the teacher has it enabled. https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen3-8B https://huggingface.co/allenai/OLMo-3-7B-Instruct 15 Preprint. Under review. As our experiments also confirm, enabling thinking mode produces substantially longer responses with greater number of epistemic tokens, making this hybrid setup function more akin to conventional teacherstudent distillation, despite using the same underlying model. We note that this configuration is inherently limited to model families such as Qwen3 that support toggling thinking mode on and off. Furthermore, for training efficiency, OPSD does not train on the entire student response; instead, it focuses only on prefix (1024 tokens by default). Unlike SDPO, which performs full fine-tuning based on verl (Sheng et al., 2024), OPSD uses LoRA fine-tuning based on trl (von Werra et al., 2020). Additionally, OPSDs hyperparameters (batch size 32, learning rate 1e-6) are smaller than those used in our setup (batch size 256, learning rate 1e-5), resulting in higher training efficiency but smaller parameter updates per step. (a) Hybrid vs. homogeneous setup performance over training. (b) Response length and epistemic token usage in the homogeneous setup. Figure 12: Training dynamics of OPSD hybrid distillation in Qwen3-1.7B compared to our homogeneous chat template setup. (a) Under the hybrid setup, the thinking-enabled teacher initially improves student performance, but gains reverse over time. In contrast, the homogeneous setup shows consistent decrease. (b) Response length and epistemic token usage in the homogeneous setup. Under this hybrid setup with prefix learning in Qwen3-1.7B, as shown in Figure 12a (orange line), we observe an intriguing training dynamic: in the early stages, the thinking-enabled teacher drives the student toward longer responses with improved performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of hybrid distillation in the early phase of training. However, as training progresses, the response length gradually decreases, accompanied by corresponding degradation in performance. In contrast, under our homogeneous setup as in Figure 12b, where both the student and the teacher have thinking mode enabled, performance declines consistently, while response length and epistemic token count also decrease steadily, consistent with our earlier analysis. While this hybrid distillation setup for the Qwen3 series presents an interesting research direction with its own unique training dynamics, such as why performance initially improves before declining and whether this stems from changes in reasoning behavior or chat template mismatch, thorough investigation is beyond the scope of this work and is left for future exploration. More On-Policy Self-Distillation Results D.1 Qwen3-8B (Thinking Mode: OFF) As an extension of Figure 5 in Section 5.3, we additionally compare AMC23 evaluation scores and the change in epistemic token usage on AIME24. As shown in Figure 13, GRPO significantly increases response length and yields substantial performance gains on both benchmarks. In contrast, SDPO exhibits divergent trends: on AMC23, acc@16 increases from 0.67 to 0.73 while reducing response length by roughly half, whereas on AIME24, acc@16 slightly decreases from 0.25 to 0.23, with pass@16 dropping more substantially. Notably on AMC23, SDPO achieves approximately 6-point improvement with shorter responses, whereas GRPO obtains much larger improvement of around 36 points at the cost of considerably longer responses. Achieving large performance gains while maintaining reasonable response length remains an open challenge. 16 Preprint. Under review. (a) Training score-length comparison (b) AMC23 score and response length (c) AIME24 score and response length (d) Change in epistemic token usage on AIME24 Figure 13: Extended results for Qwen3-8B (thinking mode off): training score-length tradeoff, evaluation performance on AMC23 and AIME24, and change in epistemic token usage. D.2 Olmo-3-7B-Instruct In addition to DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen3-8B, we further evaluate on-policy self-distillation on OLMo-3-7B-Instruct from different model family. As shown in Figure 14, consistent with our previous analyses, SDPO also degrades reasoning performance on this model, with OOD evaluation scores falling below those of the base model. This confirms that our findings are not model-dependent but reflect robust characteristics of reasoning behavior across diverse model families. (a) Training score-length comparison (b) AIME24 score and response length Figure 14: SDPO results on OLMo-3-7B-Instruct: training score and response length, and OOD evaluation on AIME24. D.3 Pass@16 Score In addition to the acc@16 scores for DeepSeek-Distill-7B and Qwen3-8B (thinking mode enabled) presented in Figures 3b, 3c, 4b, and 4c, we report pass@16 scores in Figure 15. The gap between GRPO and SDPO in pass@16 is larger for DeepSeek-Distill-7B than for Qwen3-8B, and more pronounced on the harder benchmark (AIME24) compared to AMC23. (a) DeepSeek-Distill-7B (b) Qwen3-8B (thinking mode enabled) Figure 15: Pass@16 on AMC23 and AIME24 for GRPO and SDPO across training steps. 17 Preprint. Under review.

sections.title: E More Ablation Study
sections.content: To examine the effect of various training hyperparameters on self-distillation behavior, we conduct additional experiments by varying the top-k distillation parameter and the learning rate. As shown in Figure 16a, increasing top-k from 100 to 256 yields no significant difference in training dynamics or final performance. Meanwhile, as illustrated in Figure 16b, reducing the learning rate from 1e5 to 1e6 merely slows the rate of degradation; the model ultimately converges to the same reasoning behavior. (a) Effect of top-k distillation (k = 100 vs. = 256). (b) Effect of learning rate (1e5 vs. 1e6). Figure 16: Ablation study on top-k distillation and learning rate. Both modifications fail to prevent the convergence toward degraded reasoning behavior; lower learning rate only delays the process.
